_meta.title: TCP Congestion Control — Reno, CUBIC, BBR

questions.question: During TCP slow start, how does cwnd change per RTT?
options: It stays constant
It increases by 1 MSS per RTT
It doubles each RTT
It is halved each RTT
questions.explanation: In slow start, cwnd doubles each RTT because every ACK adds 1 MSS to cwnd. With cwnd segments in flight, the sender receives cwnd ACKs per RTT, effectively doubling the window.

questions.question: What triggers fast retransmit in TCP?
options: A single packet loss detected by timeout
Three duplicate ACKs received by the sender
The receiver closing its window to zero
The sender exceeding ssthresh
questions.explanation: Fast retransmit is triggered by receiving 3 duplicate ACKs. This indicates the receiver got packets after a gap and is requesting retransmission — the network is still working, so the sender retransmits immediately without waiting for a timeout.

questions.question: What is the key difference between CUBIC and Reno's congestion avoidance phase?
options: CUBIC uses a cubic function for window growth instead of linear growth
CUBIC never enters slow start
CUBIC ignores packet loss entirely
CUBIC uses a smaller initial ssthresh
questions.explanation: CUBIC replaces Reno's linear additive increase with a cubic function W(t) = C(t-K)^3 + W_max. This allows faster recovery to the previous window after loss and better utilization on high-bandwidth links where linear growth is too slow.

questions.question: How does BBR differ fundamentally from loss-based algorithms like Reno and CUBIC?
options: BBR uses a larger initial congestion window
BBR models the network path (bandwidth + RTT) instead of reacting to packet loss
BBR disables retransmission entirely
BBR only works on local networks
questions.explanation: BBR estimates the bottleneck bandwidth (BtlBw) and minimum RTT (RTprop) to set a sending rate equal to the bandwidth-delay product. It doesn't use packet loss as a signal, which avoids filling router queues and causing bufferbloat.

questions.question: Why does TCP need congestion control?
options: To encrypt data before sending
To prevent the sender from overwhelming the network, causing packet loss and congestion collapse
To ensure packets arrive in order
To compress data for faster transmission
questions.explanation: Without congestion control, senders would transmit at full speed, overwhelming routers (which have finite buffers). This causes packet loss, retransmissions, and more congestion — a vicious cycle called congestion collapse. TCP's congestion control (Reno, CUBIC, BBR) adaptively adjusts sending rate.
